My colleagues and I once did an experiment showing how concessions lead to the tendency of others to reciprocate with their own concessions. We called people by phone and asked them to participate with no payment in a 15-minute survey for a California insurance company. As you might guess, the vast majority declined. Only 25% agreed to participate. But for another set of people who we called, we first asked them to participate in an hour-long survey for the insurance company. And then, after they refused, as they all did, we said, well, if you can't do that, would you just participate in a 15-minute survey for our company? Now, instead of 25% agreement, we got 44% of these people to participate in the 15-minute survey. So by retreating to the 15-minute request, rather than just proposing it from the outset, we nearly doubled the compliance with exactly the same request.
